title: Making a mould and deckle
subtitle: A simple wooden frame for home-studio sheet forming
excerpt: A mould and deckle is the essential tool for hand-made papermaking. The mould carries the wire or mesh that forms the sheet; the deckle is the matching upper frame that defines the sheet edges. Both can be made at home from hardwood, simple joinery, and fine-mesh aluminium or nylon screening.
sourceNotes: Mould and deckle construction documented in Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), chapter on Western European papermaking equipment. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/oldpapermaking00hunt. The basic frame construction described here follows the traditional rectangular European mould design.

content.text: is the better purchase if papermaking is likely to become a regular practice. The home-made version described here uses hardwood strips, wood glue, and aluminium window-screen mesh. It serves for twenty to fifty batches before the mesh loosens from the frame; the commercial version lasts indefinitely.

attrs.body: Use a close-grained hardwood, oak, ash, or hard maple. Softwoods swell and warp with repeated immersion in water. The frame pieces must be quarter-sawn or rift-sawn if possible, so the grain runs perpendicular to the frame face and the wood stays flat.
attrs.title: Wood selection

items.qty: 4 lengths of 225 mm + 4 lengths of 160 mm (for an A5 inside dimension of 210 mm × 148 mm)
items.name: Hardwood strip, oak or ash, 20 mm × 12 mm

items.qty: One piece slightly larger than the mould inside dimension
items.name: Aluminium window-screen mesh (or fine nylon screening), 150 DPI or finer
Waterproof wood glue (exterior PVA or Titebond III)
Heavy-duty staple gun and 6 mm staples

items.qty: Enough for two coats on the completed frames
items.name: Waterproof exterior varnish

items.qty: For covering the mesh staples at the frame edge
items.name: Thin waterproof tape (duck tape or silicone tape)

attrs.heading: Materials for an A5 mould and deckle

content.text: Cut two pairs of hardwood strips: two at 225 mm (the long sides) and two at 136 mm (the short sides, calculated as the inside dimension minus two frame widths: 160 mm minus 2 × 20 mm = 120 mm plus the halved joint). The simplest joint for a home mould is a half-lap: cut a shallow rectangular notch from the end of each piece so that long and short pieces interlock flat. The lap depth is half the frame thickness (6 mm for a 12 mm thick frame). Apply waterproof glue, clamp, and allow to cure for eight hours. Make two identical frames, one will become the mould, one the deckle.

Sand both frames to 240 grit. Varnish with two coats of exterior varnish, drying between coats. The mould frame (only) gets the mesh: stretch the aluminium or nylon screen taut across one face of the mould frame, pulling from opposite sides alternately. Staple along all four edges at 25 mm intervals. Keep the mesh taut: a slack mesh will create an uneven sheet. Trim the excess mesh to within 5 mm of the staple line, then cover the staples and mesh edge with waterproof tape. The mesh face is the underside of the mould during sheet-forming.
Using and caring for the mould and deckle

After each session, rinse the mould and deckle thoroughly in clean water. Do not leave pulp to dry on the mesh, it is very difficult to remove once dry. Store both frames flat and allow to dry completely before storage. The
on the finished sheets will be more pronounced if the deckle fits the mould firmly; a loose deckle allows more pulp to creep under the edge.
